paragraphs.body: What would you do, if you couldn’t do a thing

In the article “One Of America's Most Infamous Tornadoes, The Deadly Oklahoma City F5, Struck 25 Years Ago” the author Johnathan Erdman emphasizes how a Tornadoes damage can be catastrophic, the pain for the people afflicted unbearable and the damage imminent. By developing the following ideas, Erdman describes the dangers of F4/F5 tornadoes and other tornados within the Oklahoma City area. Firstly, Erdman describes the damages that the F5 tornado caused on May 3rd, 1999 but stating facts about the damage done. Second, Erdman compares the F5 tornado to two other F4 tornadoes that occurred that day in Oklahoma, using images he compared the damage to truly analyze the danger of an F5 compared to an F4. Lastly, Erdman using graphs and radar imaging to further express the epidemic of tornadoes within Oklahoma. Overall Erdman reports the dangers, frequency, unpredictability and overall inevitability of a tornado. This informational piece of writing is intended to inform people in Oklahoma and people interested in tornadoes within the Oklahoma area specifically. Johnathan Erdwin’s article fortunately gave me a different perspective that helped me fight off the feeling of inevitability I feared so much. Since I was young, I always felt healthy, I never missed school, my vision and hearing were spot on, in fact the worst thing that happened to me was an ankle sprain. Looking back, It’s funny how clueless a person can be when they talk about something they’ve never experienced. Whenever my mother told me what I was doing was not safe, such as when I rode a bike without a helmet or tried to parkour in public, I would never truly believe it. As if I thought there was nothing could ever happen to me. I never thought it could all change so instantly. An autumn day like any other, went on as normal. School day went by as usual, and the leaves were falling off trees on a beautiful New York autumn day. I had practice as usual, but before leaving I found myself thinking what if I just skip and sleep in, I reluctantly showed up. Practice went on as usual, it was almost over when suddenly, an elbow landed right into my nose. I feel to the ground in agony, it wasn’t long till I was told my unfortunate fate that I was concussed. For months I spent the days entrapped in my dark quiet room recovering. I missed months of school, lost connections with friends and felt hopeless. Eventually I was able to go back my daily activities, but I never felt the same, I had symptoms that I could not ignore. It felt like as if it was my inevitable fate to live like this forever. And no matte how much I healed the damage my brain suffered that day would always be prevalent. I wonder how when I look back at Johnathan Erdman’s article, I feel a sense of familiarity. And no, I’ve never been near a tornado or ever seen a tornado. This made me wonder, how could this be possible, but I noticed, it was all about how you can feel the hopeless inevitability. Sometimes you may not be able to see a tornado coming, but if it hits your area when you don’t expect it the damage can be catastrophic. That’s how my concussion felt, as if once I had been hit by that elbow I knew it was too late, it was inevitable.

paragraphs.body: I’ve been fighting the feeling that I may never be the same again, that in this one life I so must cherish I won’t feel as quick, as bright or as good as I did before. There will now be the hopeless feeling that my brain had been damaged, and I cannot do anything to reverse it. I felt like I was behind in some sort of way, as in disadvantaged. At this moment all I can do is fight feelings of dread. I felt as if the tornado did irreplaceable damage. The worst part is the more concussions you get the worse it gets. And that’s exactly what happened, a couple months later it happened again. It felt inevitable, I felt weak, and I worried it may happen again. One quote that I refer to often in the article refers to what I feared most. When the author of the article Erdman, was referring to the damage induced by the F5 tornado it often resonated with the fears I had about my concussion, the question would I ever feel the same? Often raced through my head and the quote, “Only a slab remained of this home after the May 3, 1999 tornado outbreak.” Reminded me of it. This is because I will unfortunately never know how much damage was done inside my brain , as if the slab was the outer shell of my brain but the inner walls of my brain were gone. I felt utterly hopeless. As if all that was left was a slab where I once had a beautiful home that was no more.

annotations.text: It felt inevitable, I felt weak, and I worried it may happen again.

paragraphs.body: Overtime I began to feel better. It took a long time, but things became to turn for the better. I wasn’t worried about a concussion anymore. Days began to be easier; I rekindled the friendships I had and I felt as good as new. It felt like I turned the page to a new chapter, the same way all places who had been hit by a tornado do. A quote in the article that I can relate is about the amount of damage a tornado since it correlates with the words of the doctors that cared for my concussion. The author Johnathan Erdman reminds us that even through damage there is a bright side to unfortunate events in the quote, “It could have been even worse: A violent tornado roaring into a heavily populated metro area is a frightening scenario”. In this quote Erdman points out how even through the devastating damage of a tornado, the fact that the damage wasn’t worse is something to be happy about. Similarly to a concussion the range of damage can be extremely severe and seeing I have recovered more than once to full ability is something to be happy about.

paragraphs.body: My health has been through ups and downs. Through all the struggle I have learned that I must enjoy every last moment I am healthy. To enjoy life the fullest and to not worry about the storm that past or the ones that can be ahead. Similarly to the city of Oklahoma, and the inhabited areas surrounding the city, you cannot live in the past but only recreate your future and rebuild even stronger. Looking forward I find more purpose in the things I do and become more appreciative of the time I have to express myself, healthy and happy, once again.
